narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and a Year 1 school decile of 5, you've clearly put in strong effort during your undergrad years, outperforming the typical student's average GPA of 3.21 and holding your own in early academics. However, your LSAT score of 28.50 falls notably below the dataset average of 36.70, your Year 3 decile of 4 is lower than the average of 5.53, and your family's middle-income bracket aligns with typical applicants. The university's machine learning model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 52.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass on the first try. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your LSAT score, which had a substantial negative impact compared to what the model sees in successful students. On a brighter note, your above-average undergraduate GPA strongly pulled the prediction in the favorable direction toward passing, acting as your strongest positive contributor. Your Year 1 decile ranking also helped by nudging the odds toward success, more so than your later Year 3 performance, which slightly worked against you. Your middle family income had a moderate influence tilting toward failure risk, while planning to attend full-time—common among 92.7% of students—provided a small boost toward passing. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by excelling in GPA but lagging in LSAT and later deciles, which tipped the balance for this model. While disappointing, this isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background who's already shown undergraduate grit. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that key score, strengthening your Year 3 performance if possible, and reapplying to schools with models or policies that weigh your GPA strengths more heavily. You're capable of turning this around—many students do. Keep pursuing your legal ambitions with that same determination.
